summary: Shaw argues that the programming community's ability to influence careers and products through platforms like Reddit and Hacker News has effectively ended. He uses his own experience as evidence: after Reddit removed his Python book from recommended reading lists and online mobs attacked his criticism of Python 3, his traffic and sales remained unchanged. He contends that the previous generation of programmers consistently backed terrible ideas based on tribal loyalty rather than merit, and that a newer generation of programmers is rejecting this tribal, hive-mind culture. He concludes by encouraging creators to ignore the online mob and keep making things.
key_points: Reddit's removal of Shaw's book from recommended Python resources had zero measurable impact on his sales or traffic
Python 3 has persistent usability problems for beginners—particularly around strings and error messages—that the community refuses to address, preferring to pressure authors instead
The programmer hive mind consistently backs bad technology choices based on tribal identity rather than technical merit
The newer generation of programmers is more well-rounded and less susceptible to tribal groupthink
Online outrage from platforms like HN and Reddit has far less real-world influence than people fear
money_quotes: The reddit community responsible for teaching beginners to code censored my book as a power play to get me to force Python 3 on unsuspecting beginners.
I decided to just write what I felt and fuck whatever programmers think.
Who gives a fuck about what a bunch of angry lonely coders think about my thoughts?
I've heard of VCs actually threatening to strip away funding over bad HN reactions like HN is on the same level as the food critic of the NYT.
They ran to Node.js because of 'events are better than threads' and had no idea Hoare or coroutines existed.
For all their claims of superiority for having once bought a copy of The Art of Computer Programming the previous generation of programmers are sadly uninformed about basic shit.
I believe that the influence of the previous generation of programmers is largely gone.
Keep making cool stuff and speaking your mind counter to the hive.
key_insight: The tech community's online outrage machine has far less real-world power than people believe, and creators should stop fearing it and build what they want.
